title: Classification and cladistics
summary: This lesson explores the principles of biological classification, from the traditional Linnaean system to the modern approach of cladistics. You will learn how organisms are named, grouped into a hierarchy, and how molecular evidence like DNA is used to uncover their evolutionary relationships.
learningObjectives: Outline the binomial system for naming species and the hierarchy of taxa.
Distinguish between natural classification based on ancestry and artificial classification based on superficial traits.
Explain that cladistics uses shared characteristics, particularly molecular data, to determine evolutionary relationships.
Analyse cladograms to deduce evolutionary relationships between organisms.

simpleExplanation.title: Life's Family Tree
simpleExplanation.summary: Classification is biology's way of organising all living things into a coherent system, like a giant library. Cladistics is the modern method, using DNA evidence to draw the most accurate family tree (cladogram) possible, revealing deep evolutionary connections.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're building a family tree. You and your siblings are the closest, sharing parents. Your cousins are a bit further away, sharing grandparents. Cladistics is like using a DNA ancestry test to build this tree for all of life. Instead of just looking at family photos (physical traits), it reads the genetic code to see who truly shares the most recent common ancestors.
steps: First, select the group of species you want to classify and compare.
Next, gather data on their characteristics. The most reliable data comes from comparing base sequences in DNA or amino acid sequences in proteins.
Then, count the differences between the sequences. Fewer differences imply a closer evolutionary relationship and a more recent common ancestor.
Finally, construct a cladogram. This is a branching diagram where each split (node) represents a common ancestor, and the pattern of branching shows the inferred evolutionary history.

flashcards.back: The formal system of naming species whereby each species is assigned a two-part name: the Genus name (capitalised) and the species name (lower case). For example, *Homo sapiens*.
The seven levels of classification: Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species. A useful mnemonic is 'King Philip Came Over For Good Soup'.
A method of classifying organisms into groups (clades) based on the most recent common ancestor. It relies on analysing shared, derived characteristics, primarily from molecular data.
A group of organisms that have evolved from a common ancestor. A clade includes the ancestral organism and all of its descendants.
A tree-like diagram that represents the findings of cladistics, showing the evolutionary relationships between a group of organisms.
A branching point on a cladogram that represents the divergence of two or more new groups from a common ancestor.
Structures that are similar in different species because they were inherited from a common ancestor. They may have different functions. Example: the pentadactyl limb in mammals, birds, and reptiles.
Structures that have a similar function and appearance but have evolved independently in different lineages (convergent evolution). Example: wings of an insect and a bird.
A technique that uses the mutation rate of biomolecules (like DNA) to deduce the time in prehistory when two or more life forms diverged.
A key example of reclassification. DNA evidence revealed that the traditional classification based on flower shape was incorrect, leading to the family being split into several new groups.

Do not assume that an organism at the 'top' or 'end' of a cladogram is more 'advanced'. Cladograms only show relative relatedness. The branching pattern is what matters, not the order of the terminal taxa.

faq.a: No. This is a common misconception. The cladogram shows that humans and chimpanzees share a recent common ancestor that was neither human nor chimpanzee. They are more like evolutionary cousins, having diverged from that common ancestral population around 6-7 million years ago.

faq.q: What is the difference between a cladogram and a phylogenetic tree?
faq.a: For the purposes of IB Biology SL, the terms are often used interchangeably to represent evolutionary relationships. However, in a stricter sense, a cladogram only shows the branching pattern of divergence. In some phylogenetic trees (called chronograms), the length of the branches can be proportional to the amount of time since divergence, providing an extra layer of information.

faq.q: Why is Latin used for binomial names?
faq.a: Latin was the historical language of scholarship in Europe when Linnaeus developed his system. Using a 'dead' language has the advantage that its words have fixed meanings and are not subject to the changes that occur in modern, evolving languages. This ensures stability and universality.

sections.content: With millions of species on Earth, how do biologists keep track of them all? The answer lies in classification, a systematic way of organising life. This process began with observable traits but has been revolutionised by our ability to read the genetic code itself, leading to a more accurate understanding of the tree of life.
The Linnaean System: A Universal Language

Developed by Carl Linnaeus in the 18th century, this system provides a framework for naming and grouping organisms. The cornerstone is the **binomial system**, which gives every species a unique, two-part Latin name. The first part is the genus (always capitalised) and the second is the species (always lower case), e.g., *Panthera leo* (lion). These names are universal, avoiding confusion caused by common names that vary by language and region.

Organisms are then grouped into a nested hierarchy of taxa. The seven principal levels are: **Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, and Species**. As you move down the hierarchy from kingdom to species, the groups become smaller and the organisms within them share more characteristics, indicating a more recent common ancestor.
Cladistics: Rebuilding the Tree of Life with DNA

While Linnaean classification was traditionally based on morphology (physical form), modern classification, or **cladistics**, focuses on phylogeny (evolutionary history). The fundamental principle is that organisms should be grouped based on a shared common ancestor. The most powerful evidence for this comes from molecular data.

items: Cladistics groups organisms into clades, where a clade consists of an ancestral organism and all of its descendants.
Evidence for cladistics is primarily gathered from comparing base sequences of DNA or amino acid sequences of proteins.
The number of differences in these sequences is used to estimate how long ago species diverged from a common ancestor (the molecular clock principle).
The results are presented in a diagram called a cladogram, where each branch point (node) represents a common ancestor.

sections.question:
The table shows the number of amino acid differences in the haemoglobin protein between humans and four other primate species. 

| Species | Amino Acid Differences from Human Haemoglobin |
| :--- | :--- |
| Chimpanzee | 0 |
| Gorilla | 1 |
| Gibbon | 3 |
| Rhesus Monkey | 8 |

(a) Based on the data, which species is the most closely related to humans? [1]
(b) Construct a simple cladogram to show the phylogenetic relationship between these five species. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a) [1 mark]**
Chimpanzee; as it has zero amino acid differences, indicating the most recent common ancestor with humans.

**(b) [2 marks]**
*A correctly drawn cladogram with appropriate branching order gets [2]. One mark for a partially correct order.* 

**Working:** The branching order is determined by the number of differences. Fewer differences mean a more recent split.
- Humans and Chimpanzees are the closest (0 differences), so they share the most recent node.
- The Gorilla branch splits off just before the human-chimp split (1 difference).
- The Gibbon branch splits off before that (3 differences).
- The Rhesus Monkey is the outgroup, splitting off first (8 differences).

**Cladogram:**
```
      ____________________ Rhesus Monkey
     |
     |____________________ Gibbon
     |
     |____________________ Gorilla
     |
     |__________ Human
     |
     |__________ Chimpanzee
```
*(Note: The positions of Human and Chimpanzee can be swapped, as can the entire branches at each node)*

sections.content: In an exam, when asked to draw or interpret a cladogram, remember that the branching pattern is the only information conveyed unless told otherwise. The 'left-right' order of the species at the tips is irrelevant. You can rotate the branches around a node without changing the meaning of the cladogram.
Case Study: Reclassification of the Figworts

The figwort family, Scrophulariaceae, is a classic example of how cladistics can revolutionise classification. Traditionally, this was a huge family of flowering plants grouped together based on similarities in their flower structure, such as the number of stamens. However, when botanists analysed the DNA sequences of three chloroplast genes, the data showed that the original grouping was not monophyletic (i.e., it did not contain all descendants of a single common ancestor).

The DNA evidence revealed that the old family was an artificial group, containing species that had evolved similar flower shapes through convergent evolution. As a result, the Scrophulariaceae family was drastically reorganised. Many species were moved to other existing families (like the plantain family) and some new families were created. This demonstrates that scientific classifications are hypotheses that can be falsified and improved with new evidence.

sections.question:
A cladogram is constructed for four species, W, X, Y, and Z. The node connecting W and X is the most recent. The node connecting the (W, X) clade to Y is the next most recent. Species Z is the outgroup, branching off from the base of the tree.

(a) Identify the species that is the most distant relative of species Y. [1]
(b) Identify a clade that contains species W but not species Y. [1]
sections.solution:
**(a) [1 mark]**
Species Z; as it is the outgroup and shares the most distant common ancestor with all other species in the cladogram, including Y.

**(b) [1 mark]**
The clade containing only species W and X; this clade originates from their unique common ancestor, which is not an ancestor of Y.

sections.label: Practice Questions: A3.2 Classification and cladistics
